title: MASLD / MASH
oneLiner: Metabolic liver disease (steatosis → steatohepatitis → cirrhosis); driven by obesity/insulin resistance; now the commonest cause of chronic liver disease in the UK.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Variceal haemorrhage:** massive haematemesis/melaena + shock (the patient who looks well then collapses). **Single finding:** visible blood in mouth or fresh PR blood + tachycardia.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy:** acute confusion, asterixis, or coma — may be first presentation of decompensation. **Single finding:** disoriented + flapping tremor (asterixis) + history of liver disease.
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis:** fever + abdominal pain + new or worsening ascites. **Single finding:** tender, tense ascites + fever + raised WCC on ascitic tap.
- **Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF):** rapid worsening (jaundice, coagulopathy, organ failure) — often precipitated by infection or alcohol. **Single finding:** rising INR + bilirubin + lactate with any organ failure (e.g., hypotension, oliguria, confusion).

**Rule out these before assuming stable MASLD/MASH — especially in patients with features of cirrhosis.**
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Asymptomatic (incidental finding on imaging or elevated LFTs)
  - Fatigue, right upper quadrant discomfort, vague dyspepsia
  - Later: weight loss, jaundice, pruritus, easy bruising, abdominal distension (ascites), confusion (encephalopathy), GI bleeding (varices)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hepatomegaly (firm, smooth edge)
  - Stigmata of chronic liver disease: spider naevi (>3), palmar erythema, gynaecomastia, caput medusae, Dupuytren’s contracture
  - Signs of decompensation: jaundice, ascites, splenomegaly, asterixis, confusion
  - Metabolic features: obesity (central), hypertension, acanthosis nigricans (insulin resistance)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - **Alcoholic liver disease:** heavy alcohol history (>14 units/week women, >21 men), AST:ALT >2, raised GGT out of proportion to ALT
  - **Drug-induced steatosis:** methotrexate, tamoxifen, amiodarone, valproate, corticosteroids
  - **Viral hepatitis (B, C):** positive serology, risk factors (IVDU, blood products, endemic)
  - **Autoimmune hepatitis:** positive ANA/AMA/SMA, raised IgG, younger patient, female
  - **Wilson disease:** Kayser-Fleischer rings, low ceruloplasmin, early age (<40)
  - **Haemochromatosis:** raised ferritin, transferrin saturation, features of iron overload (bronze skin, diabetes, arthropathy)
- **Splitter – alcohol vs MASLD:** AST:ALT >2 + markedly elevated GGT (often >200) → alcohol; AST:ALT <2 + mild GGT rise → MASLD. **Also:** history — use AUDIT-C or CAGE; alcohol is common comorbidity in MASLD patients so do not exclude co-existence.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** 
  - Urine dip for glucose (screen for diabetes)
  - Vital signs, including BMI and waist circumference
  - Assess for asterixis, ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill)
- **Bloods:**
  - LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin, albumin, PT/INR) – ALT usually > AST in early MASLD, AST>ALT in advanced fibrosis or alcohol
  - FBC, coagulation, U&Es, glucose, HbA1c, lipid profile
  - Non-invasive fibrosis markers: **FIB-4** (age, AST, ALT, platelets) – low <1.3 rules out advanced fibrosis; high >2.67 suggests advanced fibrosis
  - **ELF test** (Enhanced Liver Fibrosis score) – if FIB-4 intermediate or high; >9.5 suggests advanced fibrosis
  - Consider viral serology (HBsAg, anti-HCV, anti-HAV IgM), ANA/AMA/SMA, ferritin/transferrin saturation, α1-antitrypsin
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal ultrasound – assesses steatosis (bright liver), hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, ascites, focal lesions (HCC surveillance if cirrhosis)
  - **Second-line:** Transient elastography (FibroScan) – measures liver stiffness to quantify fibrosis; >8–12 kPa suggests significant fibrosis, >14 kPa likely cirrhosis; also CAP score for steatosis
  - MR elastography or liver biopsy if non-invasive tests conflicting or unclear aetiology
- **Special tests:**
  - Liver biopsy – rarely needed; reserved for suspected alternative diagnosis or when non-invasive tests equivocal after lifestyle optimisation
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **MASLD definition:** Steatosis on imaging + at least one metabolic risk factor (obesity, T2DM, hypertension, dyslipidaemia) + no significant alcohol (>14 units/week women, >21 men)
  - **FIB-4 score:** calculate online or manually: `(age × AST) / (platelets × √ALT)` – cut-offs: <1.3 low risk, 1.3–2.67 indeterminate, >2.67 high risk
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Exclude alcohol history** (AUDIT-C) – otherwise you treat for ‘MASLD’ while missing alcohol-related disease. **Also:** in any patient with abnormal LFTs, check **HBsAg and anti-HCV** before assuming MASLD — treatable viral hepatitis changes everything.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If decompensated (variceal bleed, encephalopathy, SBP) – follow specific protocols:
    - **Variceal bleed:** resuscitate, terlipressin (dose per [local policy]), antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV), early endoscopy (<12h)
    - **Hepatic encephalopathy:** lactulose (dose per [local policy]), IV thiamine (if alcohol-related), consider rifaximin if refractory
    - **SBP:** antibiotics (e.g., cefotaxime 2g IV BD or piperacillin-tazobactam if high risk) – obtain ascitic tap before antibiotics if safe
    - **ACLF:** intensive care support; treat precipitant (infection, bleeding, sepsis)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Stable MASLD: no urgent treatment
  - Assess for fibrosis stage (FIB-4, FibroScan) → guide referral
  - Start lifestyle intervention: **structured weight loss** (≥10% goal if overweight/obese) – refer to dietitian/weight management service
  - Optimise metabolic comorbidity: glycaemic control (HbA1c target <7–8% per diabetes guidelines), manage hypertension, dyslipidaemia – usually metformin, statins, ACEi/ARB as appropriate (no specific liver restrictions)
  - **Drug therapy for MASH:**
    - Consider **vitamin E 800 IU/day** (non-diabetic, biopsy-proven NASH) – off-label; discuss with seniors
    - Consider **pioglitazone 30mg OD** (in T2DM with NASH) – caution: weight gain, fluid retention, possible bladder cancer risk
    - **Resmetirom** (THR-β agonist) recently FDA-approved but not NICE recommended yet; [check local policy]
  - Vaccinate against hepatitis A, hepatitis B, pneumococcus, influenza
  - Alcohol counselling if any alcohol use (co-exists in ~20%)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to hepatology: if FIB-4>1.3 or indeterminate/high ELF, or any decompensation
  - Screen for cirrhosis complications: **6-monthly ultrasound + AFP** for HCC surveillance if cirrhosis
  - Repeat FibroScan in 1–3 years if no fibrosis; 6–12 months with fibrosis
  - Bariatric surgery referral if BMI>35 with comorbidities or >40
- **Default Orders (if admitted for decompensation):**
  - **Fluids:** 5% dextrose with KCl (avoid 0.9% saline in cirrhosis – risk of ascites exacerbation) – rate tailored to status (e.g., 1L/24h if hyponatraemia)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) – **contraindicated if active variceal bleed or coagulopathy (INR>2.0)**
  - **Diet:** low salt (<2g/day) if ascites; high calorie, protein (e.g. 1.2g/kg/day) if malnourished; NBM if variceal bleed pending endoscopy
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 (more frequent if unstable); daily weight, strict fluid balance
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Terlipressin** (for variceal bleed): 1–2mg IV q4–6h for up to 5 days – [check local protocol]
  - **Octreotide** (50mcg IV bolus then 50mcg/hour infusion) – alternative if terlipressin not available
  - **Lactulose**: 15–30mL orally BD–QDS, titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day; or 200–300mL PR if encephalopathy
  - **Rifaximin** (for secondary prophylaxis of HE): 550mg orally BD – usually added after 2nd encephalopathy episode
  - **Cefotaxime** (SBP): 2g IV BD (or ceftriaxone 1–2g IV OD)
  - **Vitamin E** (off-label): 800 IU orally OD – only non-diabetic biopsy-proven MASH
  - **Pioglitazone**: 30mg orally OD (check local diabetes protocol)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Norfloxacin (SBP prophylaxis): 400mg orally OD – [check local policy]
  - Propranolol/carvedilol (variceal bleed prophylaxis) – cardioselective beta-blocker, titrate to HR
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **NSAIDs:** avoid (GI bleed, AKI in cirrhosis)
  - **Paracetamol:** limit to 2g/day (risk of hepatotoxicity with impaired metabolism)
  - **Statins:** generally safe even in chronic liver disease, but caution in decompensated cirrhosis (check with senior)
  - **Metformin:** safe in stable cirrhosis, but avoid in decompensated (lactic acidosis risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Many drugs require dose reduction in cirrhosis (e.g., opioids, benzodiazepines, antimicrobials) – seek pharmacist advice
  - **Lactulose:** can cause hypernatraemia in high doses; monitor U&Es
  - **Terlipressin:** may worsen hyponatraemia and cause bradycardia; use with caution in renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving a high dose of benzodiazepines** (e.g., diazepam 10mg) for sedation – can precipitate hepatic encephalopathy and respiratory depression. Use **limited dose midazolam** (e.g., 1–2mg) or **no sedation** if possible.
  - **Using NSAIDs** for pain – causes acute kidney injury and GI bleeding in cirrhosis. Always prescribe paracetamol (max 2g/day) or codeine with caution.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Variceal haemorrhage (mortality 15–20% per bleed)
  - Hepatic encephalopathy (may be precipitated by infection, constipation, GI bleed, electrolyte disturbance)
  - Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (high mortality if untreated)
  - Acute kidney injury (hepatorenal syndrome)
  - Ascites (tense, refractory)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cirrhosis (compensated → decompensated)
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)
  - Cardiovascular disease (MASLD is independent risk factor)
  - Type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease
sections.Prognosis:
- Early steatosis is reversible with ≥10% weight loss; fibrosis may also regress.
- In MASH, ~20–30% progress to cirrhosis over 10–20 years.
- Once cirrhosis develops, 5-year risk of decompensation ~50–60%; once decompensated, median survival 2–3 years without transplant.
- Risk of HCC 10–15% over 5 years in MASLD cirrhosis (lower than viral hepatitis but still significant).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do** calculate FIB-4 in every patient with elevated LFTs and metabolic risk – it’s free, non-invasive, and triages need for hepatology referral.
- **Do** refer all patients with FIB-4 >1.3 or FibroScan >8 kPa to weight management programme – lifestyle is the most effective treatment.
- **Do** screen for diabetes and CVD in all MASLD patients: check HbA1c, lipids, blood pressure; treat aggressively.
- **Do** vaccinate against hepatitis A and B in all with chronic liver disease – protects against acute decompensation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t** stop statins or metformin in stable MASLD – they are safe and reduce CV mortality.
- **Don’t** rely on normal LFTs to rule out advanced fibrosis – many with cirrhosis have normal transaminases; use FIB-4.
- **Don’t** forget: a patient with ‘MASLD’ may also have alcohol-related disease – always take a thorough alcohol history.
- **Don’t** prescribe vitamin E or pioglitazone without senior discussion – they are off-label and have side effects.
- **Don’t** perform liver biopsy routinely – only if non-invasive tests are discordant or suspect alternative diagnosis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** 
  - Yellowing of skin/eyes (jaundice)
  - Vomiting blood or black/tarry stools
  - New confusion, drowsiness, or asterixis
  - Rapid increase in abdominal girth or unexplained weight gain (ascites)
  - Fever with abdominal pain (possible SBP)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP for weight management, diabetes/CVD screening
  - Hepatology if FIB-4 >1.3 or FibroScan >8 kPa – appointment within 4–8 weeks
  - If cirrhosis: 6-monthly ultrasound + AFP for HCC surveillance
  - Repeat FIB-4/FibroScan in 1 year if no fibrosis; 6–12 months if fibrosis present
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (CT1/SpR) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or >2 above baseline
  - New confusion or asterixis (possible encephalopathy)
  - Any GI bleed (haematemesis, melena) – urgent senior review and arrange endoscopy
  - Fever + ascites (possible SBP) – ascitic tap needed
  - AKI (creatinine rise >26.5µmol/L in 48h) – suspect hepatorenal syndrome

rawMarkdown:
## MASLD / MASH

**One-liner:** Metabolic liver disease (steatosis → steatohepatitis → cirrhosis); driven by obesity/insulin resistance; now the commonest cause of chronic liver disease in the UK.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Variceal haemorrhage:** massive haematemesis/melaena + shock (the patient who looks well then collapses). **Single finding:** visible blood in mouth or fresh PR blood + tachycardia.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy:** acute confusion, asterixis, or coma — may be first presentation of decompensation. **Single finding:** disoriented + flapping tremor (asterixis) + history of liver disease.
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis:** fever + abdominal pain + new or worsening ascites. **Single finding:** tender, tense ascites + fever + raised WCC on ascitic tap.
- **Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF):** rapid worsening (jaundice, coagulopathy, organ failure) — often precipitated by infection or alcohol. **Single finding:** rising INR + bilirubin + lactate with any organ failure (e.g., hypotension, oliguria, confusion).

**Rule out these before assuming stable MASLD/MASH — especially in patients with features of cirrhosis.**

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Asymptomatic (incidental finding on imaging or elevated LFTs)
  - Fatigue, right upper quadrant discomfort, vague dyspepsia
  - Later: weight loss, jaundice, pruritus, easy bruising, abdominal distension (ascites), confusion (encephalopathy), GI bleeding (varices)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hepatomegaly (firm, smooth edge)
  - Stigmata of chronic liver disease: spider naevi (>3), palmar erythema, gynaecomastia, caput medusae, Dupuytren’s contracture
  - Signs of decompensation: jaundice, ascites, splenomegaly, asterixis, confusion
  - Metabolic features: obesity (central), hypertension, acanthosis nigricans (insulin resistance)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - **Alcoholic liver disease:** heavy alcohol history (>14 units/week women, >21 men), AST:ALT >2, raised GGT out of proportion to ALT
  - **Drug-induced steatosis:** methotrexate, tamoxifen, amiodarone, valproate, corticosteroids
  - **Viral hepatitis (B, C):** positive serology, risk factors (IVDU, blood products, endemic)
  - **Autoimmune hepatitis:** positive ANA/AMA/SMA, raised IgG, younger patient, female
  - **Wilson disease:** Kayser-Fleischer rings, low ceruloplasmin, early age (<40)
  - **Haemochromatosis:** raised ferritin, transferrin saturation, features of iron overload (bronze skin, diabetes, arthropathy)
- **Splitter – alcohol vs MASLD:** AST:ALT >2 + markedly elevated GGT (often >200) → alcohol; AST:ALT <2 + mild GGT rise → MASLD. **Also:** history — use AUDIT-C or CAGE; alcohol is common comorbidity in MASLD patients so do not exclude co-existence.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** 
  - Urine dip for glucose (screen for diabetes)
  - Vital signs, including BMI and waist circumference
  - Assess for asterixis, ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill)
- **Bloods:**
  - LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin, albumin, PT/INR) – ALT usually > AST in early MASLD, AST>ALT in advanced fibrosis or alcohol
  - FBC, coagulation, U&Es, glucose, HbA1c, lipid profile
  - Non-invasive fibrosis markers: **FIB-4** (age, AST, ALT, platelets) – low <1.3 rules out advanced fibrosis; high >2.67 suggests advanced fibrosis
  - **ELF test** (Enhanced Liver Fibrosis score) – if FIB-4 intermediate or high; >9.5 suggests advanced fibrosis
  - Consider viral serology (HBsAg, anti-HCV, anti-HAV IgM), ANA/AMA/SMA, ferritin/transferrin saturation, α1-antitrypsin
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal ultrasound – assesses steatosis (bright liver), hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, ascites, focal lesions (HCC surveillance if cirrhosis)
  - **Second-line:** Transient elastography (FibroScan) – measures liver stiffness to quantify fibrosis; >8–12 kPa suggests significant fibrosis, >14 kPa likely cirrhosis; also CAP score for steatosis
  - MR elastography or liver biopsy if non-invasive tests conflicting or unclear aetiology
- **Special tests:**
  - Liver biopsy – rarely needed; reserved for suspected alternative diagnosis or when non-invasive tests equivocal after lifestyle optimisation
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **MASLD definition:** Steatosis on imaging + at least one metabolic risk factor (obesity, T2DM, hypertension, dyslipidaemia) + no significant alcohol (>14 units/week women, >21 men)
  - **FIB-4 score:** calculate online or manually: `(age × AST) / (platelets × √ALT)` – cut-offs: <1.3 low risk, 1.3–2.67 indeterminate, >2.67 high risk
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Exclude alcohol history** (AUDIT-C) – otherwise you treat for ‘MASLD’ while missing alcohol-related disease. **Also:** in any patient with abnormal LFTs, check **HBsAg and anti-HCV** before assuming MASLD — treatable viral hepatitis changes everything.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If decompensated (variceal bleed, encephalopathy, SBP) – follow specific protocols:
    - **Variceal bleed:** resuscitate, terlipressin (dose per [local policy]), antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV), early endoscopy (<12h)
    - **Hepatic encephalopathy:** lactulose (dose per [local policy]), IV thiamine (if alcohol-related), consider rifaximin if refractory
    - **SBP:** antibiotics (e.g., cefotaxime 2g IV BD or piperacillin-tazobactam if high risk) – obtain ascitic tap before antibiotics if safe
    - **ACLF:** intensive care support; treat precipitant (infection, bleeding, sepsis)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Stable MASLD: no urgent treatment
  - Assess for fibrosis stage (FIB-4, FibroScan) → guide referral
  - Start lifestyle intervention: **structured weight loss** (≥10% goal if overweight/obese) – refer to dietitian/weight management service
  - Optimise metabolic comorbidity: glycaemic control (HbA1c target <7–8% per diabetes guidelines), manage hypertension, dyslipidaemia – usually metformin, statins, ACEi/ARB as appropriate (no specific liver restrictions)
  - **Drug therapy for MASH:**
    - Consider **vitamin E 800 IU/day** (non-diabetic, biopsy-proven NASH) – off-label; discuss with seniors
    - Consider **pioglitazone 30mg OD** (in T2DM with NASH) – caution: weight gain, fluid retention, possible bladder cancer risk
    - **Resmetirom** (THR-β agonist) recently FDA-approved but not NICE recommended yet; [check local policy]
  - Vaccinate against hepatitis A, hepatitis B, pneumococcus, influenza
  - Alcohol counselling if any alcohol use (co-exists in ~20%)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to hepatology: if FIB-4>1.3 or indeterminate/high ELF, or any decompensation
  - Screen for cirrhosis complications: **6-monthly ultrasound + AFP** for HCC surveillance if cirrhosis
  - Repeat FibroScan in 1–3 years if no fibrosis; 6–12 months with fibrosis
  - Bariatric surgery referral if BMI>35 with comorbidities or >40
- **Default Orders (if admitted for decompensation):**
  - **Fluids:** 5% dextrose with KCl (avoid 0.9% saline in cirrhosis – risk of ascites exacerbation) – rate tailored to status (e.g., 1L/24h if hyponatraemia)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) – **contraindicated if active variceal bleed or coagulopathy (INR>2.0)**
  - **Diet:** low salt (<2g/day) if ascites; high calorie, protein (e.g. 1.2g/kg/day) if malnourished; NBM if variceal bleed pending endoscopy
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 (more frequent if unstable); daily weight, strict fluid balance

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Terlipressin** (for variceal bleed): 1–2mg IV q4–6h for up to 5 days – [check local protocol]
  - **Octreotide** (50mcg IV bolus then 50mcg/hour infusion) – alternative if terlipressin not available
  - **Lactulose**: 15–30mL orally BD–QDS, titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day; or 200–300mL PR if encephalopathy
  - **Rifaximin** (for secondary prophylaxis of HE): 550mg orally BD – usually added after 2nd encephalopathy episode
  - **Cefotaxime** (SBP): 2g IV BD (or ceftriaxone 1–2g IV OD)
  - **Vitamin E** (off-label): 800 IU orally OD – only non-diabetic biopsy-proven MASH
  - **Pioglitazone**: 30mg orally OD (check local diabetes protocol)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Norfloxacin (SBP prophylaxis): 400mg orally OD – [check local policy]
  - Propranolol/carvedilol (variceal bleed prophylaxis) – cardioselective beta-blocker, titrate to HR
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **NSAIDs:** avoid (GI bleed, AKI in cirrhosis)
  - **Paracetamol:** limit to 2g/day (risk of hepatotoxicity with impaired metabolism)
  - **Statins:** generally safe even in chronic liver disease, but caution in decompensated cirrhosis (check with senior)
  - **Metformin:** safe in stable cirrhosis, but avoid in decompensated (lactic acidosis risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Many drugs require dose reduction in cirrhosis (e.g., opioids, benzodiazepines, antimicrobials) – seek pharmacist advice
  - **Lactulose:** can cause hypernatraemia in high doses; monitor U&Es
  - **Terlipressin:** may worsen hyponatraemia and cause bradycardia; use with caution in renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving a high dose of benzodiazepines** (e.g., diazepam 10mg) for sedation – can precipitate hepatic encephalopathy and respiratory depression. Use **limited dose midazolam** (e.g., 1–2mg) or **no sedation** if possible.
  - **Using NSAIDs** for pain – causes acute kidney injury and GI bleeding in cirrhosis. Always prescribe paracetamol (max 2g/day) or codeine with caution.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Variceal haemorrhage (mortality 15–20% per bleed)
  - Hepatic encephalopathy (may be precipitated by infection, constipation, GI bleed, electrolyte disturbance)
  - Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (high mortality if untreated)
  - Acute kidney injury (hepatorenal syndrome)
  - Ascites (tense, refractory)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cirrhosis (compensated → decompensated)
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)
  - Cardiovascular disease (MASLD is independent risk factor)
  - Type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease

### Prognosis
- Early steatosis is reversible with ≥10% weight loss; fibrosis may also regress.
- In MASH, ~20–30% progress to cirrhosis over 10–20 years.
- Once cirrhosis develops, 5-year risk of decompensation ~50–60%; once decompensated, median survival 2–3 years without transplant.
- Risk of HCC 10–15% over 5 years in MASLD cirrhosis (lower than viral hepatitis but still significant).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do** calculate FIB-4 in every patient with elevated LFTs and metabolic risk – it’s free, non-invasive, and triages need for hepatology referral.
- **Do** refer all patients with FIB-4 >1.3 or FibroScan >8 kPa to weight management programme – lifestyle is the most effective treatment.
- **Do** screen for diabetes and CVD in all MASLD patients: check HbA1c, lipids, blood pressure; treat aggressively.
- **Do** vaccinate against hepatitis A and B in all with chronic liver disease – protects against acute decompensation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t** stop statins or metformin in stable MASLD – they are safe and reduce CV mortality.
- **Don’t** rely on normal LFTs to rule out advanced fibrosis – many with cirrhosis have normal transaminases; use FIB-4.
- **Don’t** forget: a patient with ‘MASLD’ may also have alcohol-related disease – always take a thorough alcohol history.
- **Don’t** prescribe vitamin E or pioglitazone without senior discussion – they are off-label and have side effects.
- **Don’t** perform liver biopsy routinely – only if non-invasive tests are discordant or suspect alternative diagnosis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** 
  - Yellowing of skin/eyes (jaundice)
  - Vomiting blood or black/tarry stools
  - New confusion, drowsiness, or asterixis
  - Rapid increase in abdominal girth or unexplained weight gain (ascites)
  - Fever with abdominal pain (possible SBP)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP for weight management, diabetes/CVD screening
  - Hepatology if FIB-4 >1.3 or FibroScan >8 kPa – appointment within 4–8 weeks
  - If cirrhosis: 6-monthly ultrasound + AFP for HCC surveillance
  - Repeat FIB-4/FibroScan in 1 year if no fibrosis; 6–12 months if fibrosis present

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (CT1/SpR) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or >2 above baseline
  - New confusion or asterixis (possible encephalopathy)
  - Any GI bleed (haematemesis, melena) – urgent senior review and arrange endoscopy
  - Fever + ascites (possible SBP) – ascitic tap needed
  - AKI (creatinine rise >26.5µmol/L in 48h) – suspect hepatorenal syndrome
